review.method: Item-by-item review of the official AQA 8463/1H June 2024 question paper and mark scheme, the stored D1 prompt/chain/mark rows, and the hashed local AQA Physics specification; no keyword classifier or model call.
review.boundary: Questions map only where the official prompt and positive mark evidence fix one learner-selectable Physics chapter. A context-only percentage calculation and three standalone working-scientifically measurement-error operations are explicitly withheld.

question.promptText: Which energy store increases when water is heated?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Which energy store increases when water is heated?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Thermal Store
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: heating -> particle energy -> thermal store

markSchemeItems.text: Thermal energy store or internal energy store; alternatively, kinetic energy of the water particles.
Allow Ek. Ignore 'heat'.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Which energy store increases when water is pumped uphill into a reservoir?
question.selfContainedPromptText: Which energy store increases when water is pumped uphill into a reservoir?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Raised Water
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: water raised -> greater height -> GPE store

markSchemeItems.text: Gravitational potential energy store.
Allow Ep or GPE; the mark scheme also allows kinetic energy or Ek.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Compare the advantages and disadvantages of the two methods of storing energy. Include calculations in your answer.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Table 1 gives two energy-storage methods. Method A increases the temperature of 100 kg of water by 80 °C, stores 33 600 kJ, wastes 40% of the stored energy, and can be installed anywhere. Method B pumps 100 kg of water uphill to a height of 500 m, stores 490 kJ, wastes 25% of the stored energy, and requires high mountains. Compare the advantages and disadvantages of the two methods of storing energy. Include calculations in your answer.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Storage Comparison
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: both methods -> relevant calculation -> compare trade-offs -> supported comparison

markSchemeItems.text: Identifies scientifically relevant advantages or disadvantages of Method A, such as installation anywhere, much greater stored energy, or the need for insulation.
markSchemeItems.sourceRef: Mark scheme p7, 01.3 level-of-response and indicative content

markSchemeItems.text: Identifies scientifically relevant advantages or disadvantages of Method B, such as higher efficiency or the need for a suitable high-mountain location.
markSchemeItems.sourceRef: Mark scheme p7, 01.3 level-of-response and indicative content

markSchemeItems.text: Uses the data in a relevant calculation: for example A has 60% efficiency, 20 160 kJ useful energy or 13 440 kJ wasted; B has 75% efficiency, 367.5 kJ useful energy or 122.5 kJ wasted.
Makes the similarities or differences clear and, where appropriate, notes their magnitude; a top-level comparison uses a relevant calculation to compare the methods.
Apply the official holistic level-of-response judgement: Level 2 is 3–4 marks, Level 1 is 1–2 marks, and no relevant content is 0 marks.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Explain one change that would reduce the amount of carbon dioxide released by each activity.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Decreasing the amount of carbon dioxide released by different activities will help slow down climate change. Transport and generating electricity are the two activities that released the largest amounts of carbon dioxide in the UK in 2018. Explain one change that would reduce the amount of carbon dioxide released by each activity.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Carbon Cuts
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: fossil fuel use -> reduce activity -> low-carbon alternative -> lower emissions

markSchemeItems.text: For transport, states stopping or reducing the use/burning of petrol- or diesel-powered transport (or another valid carbon-emitting transport method).
For transport, gives a lower-carbon alternative such as electric or hydrogen-fuelled cars, cycling, public transport, or walking.
For generating electricity, states stopping or reducing the use of coal, oil, or gas (fossil fuels), or reducing unnecessary electrical-appliance use.
For generating electricity, gives a lower-carbon alternative such as a renewable energy resource or nuclear power, or links lower demand to less fossil-fuel generation.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Complete the sentences. Choose answers from the box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The process of nuclear fission is used in nuclear power stations. Figure 2 shows a black particle entering a circle labelled Uranium; two unlabelled parts and three black particles leave. Complete the three printed sentences by choosing from: electrons, gamma rays, neutrons, nuclei, protons. In nuclear power stations, energy is released from uranium [blank]. The uranium in Figure 2 splits into two parts and releases three [blank]. The process of nuclear fission releases electromagnetic radiation in the form of [blank].

primaryAnswerChain.title: Fission Pattern
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: fission core -> chain particle -> EM radiation

markSchemeItems.text: First blank: nuclei.
Second blank: neutrons.
Third blank: gamma rays.
Answers must be in this order.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Write down the equation which links energy (E), power (P) and time (t).
question.selfContainedPromptText: The supplied Physics Equations Sheet gives: energy transferred = power × time. Write down the equation which links energy (E), power (P) and time (t).

primaryAnswerChain.title: Power equation
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: energy/work -> divide by time -> power

markSchemeItems.text: energy = power × time, or E = P × t.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: A nuclear power station has a power output of 500 MW. Calculate the energy output in 3600 s. Give your answer in J.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Use the Physics Equations Sheet relation energy transferred = power × time (E = Pt). A nuclear power station has a power output of 500 MW. Calculate the energy output in 3600 s. Give your answer in J.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Energy Output
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: convert power -> E=Pt -> multiply -> energy unit

markSchemeItems.text: Converts the power: P = 500 000 000 W.
Substitutes E = 500 000 000 × 3600.
Allow correct substitution and a final answer consistent with an incorrectly or unconverted value of P.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: Radioactive waste produced by nuclear power stations has a long half-life. Suggest one precaution taken to reduce the hazard caused by radioactive waste from power stations.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Radioactive waste produced by nuclear power stations has a long half-life. Suggest one precaution taken to reduce the hazard caused by radioactive waste from power stations.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Waste Safety
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: long-lived waste -> contain/shield/isolate -> reduced exposure

markSchemeItems.text: Any one valid precaution: bury the waste; put it in cooling ponds; transport it in secure vessels; store it in metal containers; or cover it in concrete.
Allow storing it for at least one half-life. Ignore references to waste level and merely labelling it hazardous.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: The student plotted a graph of resistance against the length of wire. Describe a method the student could have used to collect the data needed to plot the graph.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated how the length of a wire affects the resistance of the wire at constant temperature. Figure 3 shows a circuit containing a cell, switch, ammeter and resistor in series with a wire held along a ruler between crocodile clips; a voltmeter is connected across the measured length of wire. The student plotted a graph of resistance against the length of wire. Describe a method the student could have used to collect the data needed to plot the graph.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Wire Method
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: vary measured length -> current + voltage -> R=V/I -> repeat + mean -> control heating
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Measure both meters at each length.

markSchemeItems.text: Measures the length of wire between the crocodile clips using the ruler and varies the length by moving a crocodile clip.
Measures current with the ammeter for each chosen length.
Measures potential difference with the voltmeter for each chosen length.
Calculates resistance for each length using V = IR, equivalently R = V/I, and records resistance against length.
Improves reliability by repeating current and potential-difference readings for each length, calculating means, and dealing with anomalous readings.
Maintains constant temperature by keeping current low and/or disconnecting the circuit between readings, with the key steps logically sequenced to produce a valid outcome.
markSchemeItems.sourceRef: Mark scheme p11, 03.1 level 3 and indicative content

markSchemeItems.text: Apply the official holistic rubric: Level 3 = 5–6, Level 2 = 3–4, Level 1 = 1–2, no relevant content = 0. Level 2 requires varying wire length plus the measurements/equipment needed for potential difference and current.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Which graph shows the relationship between the resistance of a wire at constant temperature and its length? Tick one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Which of the three displayed graphs shows the relationship between the resistance of a wire at constant temperature and its length? Tick one box. The options are: a straight line decreasing with length, a curve rising from the origin and levelling off, and a straight line rising from the origin.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Direct Proportion
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: length-resistance graph -> straight line -> through origin -> direct proportion

markSchemeItems.text: Selects the bottom (third) graph: a straight line with positive gradient through the origin.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The student used a cell that had a potential difference of 1.50 V. Explain why the cell was not an electrical hazard to the student in the investigation.
question.selfContainedPromptText: In an investigation of how wire length affects resistance, the student used a cell that had a potential difference of 1.50 V. Explain why the cell was not an electrical hazard to the student in the investigation.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Low-voltage Safety
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: very low p.d. -> low shock risk

markSchemeItems.text: The potential difference is very low.
Therefore there is no risk, or less risk, of electric shock/electrocution; alternatively, the wire will not melt or get hot.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Explain why the student’s hair stands up on end.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 5 shows a student touching the metal dome of a static electricity generator. The dome is negatively charged. Explain why the student’s hair stands up on end.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Static Hair
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: electrons transfer -> same negative charge -> like charges repel -> hairs separate

markSchemeItems.text: Electrons are transferred to the student.
Her hair becomes negatively charged, or each hair has the same negative charge.
Like charges repel, so the hairs repel one another.
Do not accept the student being positively charged for the first two marking points.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: What is an electric field?
question.selfContainedPromptText: A charged metal dome creates an electric field. What is an electric field?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Electric Field
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: charged object -> surrounding region -> force on charge
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: A field is a force region.

markSchemeItems.text: The region around a charged object where another charged object experiences a force.
Allow space or area for region, and particle for object.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: How does the electric field strength vary as the distance from the charged metal dome increases?
question.selfContainedPromptText: A charged metal dome creates an electric field. How does the electric field strength vary as the distance from the charged metal dome increases?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Field Distance
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: distance increases -> field strength decreases

markSchemeItems.text: The electric field strength decreases.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The spark transfers 0.60 J of energy, and 2.0 μC of charge is transferred from the dome to the earthed conductor. Calculate the potential difference between the metal dome and the earthed conductor. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 6 shows a negatively charged metal dome and an earthed conductor. When the earthed conductor is moved towards the metal dome, there is a spark between them. The spark transfers 0.60 J of energy, and 2.0 μC of charge is transferred from the dome to the earthed conductor. Use energy transferred = charge flow × potential difference (E = QV) to calculate the potential difference between the metal dome and the earthed conductor.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Potential Difference
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: convert charge -> E=QV -> rearrange V -> calculate

markSchemeItems.text: Converts charge: Q = 2 × 10^-6 C.
Substitutes 0.6 = 2 × 10^-6 × V.
Rearranges to V = 0.6/(2 × 10^-6).
Calculates V = 300 000 V.
Allow correct substitution, rearrangement and a final answer consistent with an incorrectly or unconverted Q.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Which of the following changes would increase the distance a spark can jump between the dome and the earthed conductor? Tick one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A spark can jump between a charged metal dome and an earthed conductor. Which of the following changes would increase the distance the spark can jump? Tick one box: Decreased charge on the metal dome; Decreased electric field strength; Decreased electrical resistance of air; Decreased potential difference.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Spark Gap
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: easier air conduction -> breakdown farther away -> longer spark gap

markSchemeItems.text: Decreased electrical resistance of air.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The vending machine is connected to the mains electricity supply. What is the frequency and the potential difference of the mains electricity supply in the UK?
question.selfContainedPromptText: A vending machine is connected to the mains electricity supply. What is the frequency and the potential difference of the mains electricity supply in the UK?

primaryAnswerChain.title: UK mains
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: UK supply -> potential difference -> frequency

markSchemeItems.text: Frequency = 50 Hz.
Potential difference = 230 V.
Answers are marked in this printed order.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The power dissipated by the coin is 340 mW when the current in the coin is 0.75 A. Calculate the resistance of the coin. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A vending machine identifies the value of a coin by measuring the resistance of the coin. The power dissipated by the coin is 340 mW when the current in the coin is 0.75 A. Use the Physics Equations Sheet relation power = (current)^2 × resistance (P = I^2R) to calculate the resistance of the coin.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Resistance from Power
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: convert power -> P=I²R -> rearrange R -> calculate
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Convert power, then divide by current squared.

markSchemeItems.text: Converts 340 mW to 0.34 W.
Substitutes 0.34 = 0.75^2 × R.
Rearranges to R = 0.34 / 0.75^2.
Calculates R = 0.60 Ω; a correct value to more than 2 significant figures is also allowed.
Allow correct substitution, rearrangement and an answer consistent with an incorrectly or unconverted value of P.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Coins that are dirty are not recognised by the vending machine. Suggest one reason why.
question.selfContainedPromptText: The vending machine identifies the value of a coin by measuring the resistance of the coin. Coins that are dirty are not recognised by the vending machine. Suggest one reason why.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Coin Recognition
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: dirt layer -> resistance changes -> expected value mismatch -> coin rejected
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Recognition depends on the measured resistance.

markSchemeItems.text: The dirt changes the measured resistance of the coin, so it differs from the expected resistance or no longer matches a known coin.
Allow that dirt stops charge flow or stops the current through the coin.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The circuit symbol for a thermistor has not been included. Draw the circuit symbol for a thermistor in the box below.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 8 shows part of a circuit used to monitor the temperature inside a vending machine. It has a 12 V supply, a 400 Ω resistor in series with a missing thermistor symbol, and a voltmeter connected across the thermistor position. Draw the circuit symbol for a thermistor in the box provided.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Thermistor recall
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: temperature sensing -> changing resistance -> thermistor -> diagonal symbol

markSchemeItems.text: Draws the standard thermistor circuit symbol: a resistor rectangle crossed diagonally by a line with a short angled section.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The cooling system inside the vending machine turns on when the temperature of the thermistor is above 20 °C. Determine the potential difference across the thermistor when the temperature is 20 °C. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 8 shows a 12 V series circuit containing a 400 Ω resistor and a thermistor, with a voltmeter across the thermistor. Figure 9 is a graph of thermistor resistance in Ω against temperature in °C. The cooling system turns on when the thermistor temperature is above 20 °C. Use the graph and the relation potential difference = current × resistance (V = IR) to determine the potential difference across the thermistor when the temperature is 20 °C.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Potential Divider
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: read component resistance -> total resistance -> current/ratio -> component p.d.
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Find the component share of the supply.

markSchemeItems.text: Finds total resistance: R_total = 400 + 80 = 480 Ω (allow a thermistor reading of 70–90 Ω, giving total 470–490 Ω).
Uses 12 = I × 480, or a correct substitution/rearrangement with the allowed total-resistance range.
Calculates the circuit current as 0.025 A (or a correct value using an allowed total resistance).
Uses V_thermistor = I × R_thermistor with the calculated current and a graph reading of 70–90 Ω.
Calculates 2.0 V; allow an answer from 1.8 V to 2.2 V.
The official alternative ratio method may also receive full credit: thermistor:total resistance = 80:480 = 1:6, then V = (1/6) × 12 = 2.0 V, with the same graph-reading range.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Which energy store increases as the bungee cords are stretched?
question.selfContainedPromptText: On a theme-park ride, a pod is attached to two bungee cords that behave like springs. Which energy store increases as the bungee cords are stretched?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Elastic Store
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: stretch cord -> elastic store rises

markSchemeItems.text: Elastic potential energy store.
Allow Ee or EPE.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: When the pod is released, the pod accelerates upwards. Before the pod is released the extension of each of the two bungee cords is 8.0 m. The spring constant of each bungee cord is 735 N/m. The mass of the pod is 240 kg. Gravitational field strength = 9.8 N/kg. Calculate the maximum height reached by the pod. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A theme-park pod is attached to two bungee cords that behave like springs. When released, the pod accelerates upwards. Before release, the extension of each cord is 8.0 m, the spring constant of each cord is 735 N/m, the pod mass is 240 kg, and gravitational field strength is 9.8 N/kg. Use elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension² and gravitational potential energy = mass × gravitational field strength × height to calculate the maximum height reached by the pod.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Elastic to Height
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: elastic energy each -> account for all -> equate to mgh -> rearrange height -> calculate

markSchemeItems.text: For one cord, uses Ee = 0.5 × 735 × 8.0².
Calculates the elastic potential energy of one cord as 23 520 J.
Calculates total elastic potential energy for the two cords as 47 040 J.
Equates energy to gravitational potential energy: 47 040 = 240 × 9.8 × h.
Rearranges to h = 47 040/(240 × 9.8).
Calculates h = 20 m.
The mark scheme gives specific allowances for certain substitutions using combined spring constants/extensions and applies error-carried-forward from a student's Ee value for later stages.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: The actual maximum height reached by the pod will be lower than the correct answer to Question 06.2. Explain why.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A theme-park pod is launched upward by two stretched bungee cords. An ideal calculation assumes that all of the cords’ elastic potential energy becomes the pod’s gravitational potential energy. The actual maximum height will be lower than the ideal calculated value. Explain why.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Real Launch Height
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: air resistance -> energy dissipated -> less GPE -> lower height

markSchemeItems.text: Air resistance opposes the upward motion of the pod.
Not all elastic potential energy is transferred to gravitational potential energy; the transfer is not 100% efficient, or some energy is dissipated/transferred to the surroundings.
Ignore merely saying energy is wasted, and ignore reference to the mass of the person in the pod.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.1 Energy.

question.promptText: The student tied a piece of thick string to the metal ring and lowered the ring into the water. Suggest one reason why the student should have used thin string instead of thick string.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student measured the volume of a metal ring by lowering it into water in a measuring cylinder. The student tied a piece of thick string to the ring and lowered the ring into the water. Suggest one reason why the student should have used thin string instead of thick string.

primaryAnswerChain.title: String Displacement
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: thinner string -> less water displaced -> smaller volume error

markSchemeItems.text: Thin string would affect the volume measurement less than thick string, or would displace less water.
Ignore absorption of water by the string.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: The student determined that the density of the ring was 21 500 kg/m³. The volume of the ring was 0.44 cm³. Calculate the mass of the ring. Use the Physics Equations Sheet. Give your answer in kg.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A metal ring has density 21 500 kg/m³ and volume 0.44 cm³. Use the Physics Equations Sheet relation density = mass/volume to calculate the ring's mass. Give your answer in kg.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Mass from Density
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: convert volume -> ρ=m/V -> rearrange m -> calculate

markSchemeItems.text: Converts 0.44 cm³ to 4.4 × 10^-7 m³.
Rearranges to m = 21 500 × 4.4 × 10^-7.
Calculates m = 0.00946 kg or 9.46 × 10^-3 kg.
Allow correct substitution, rearrangement and a final answer consistent with an incorrectly or unconverted volume.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: When the plunger was pushed slowly into the syringe, the pressure in the syringe increased. The temperature of the air remained constant. Explain why the pressure increased.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student investigated how pressure varies with volume for a fixed mass of air in a sealed syringe connected to a pressure gauge. When the plunger was pushed slowly into the syringe, the pressure increased while the air temperature remained constant. Explain why the pressure increased.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Gas Pressure
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: temperature/density -> particle motion -> wall collisions -> pressure change

markSchemeItems.text: The air particles are closer together.
The frequency of collisions between the air particles and the syringe walls increases.
There is a larger total force on a smaller surface area, or a larger force per unit area.

Ignore references to increased kinetic energy or concentration. Do not credit the collision-frequency point if it is linked to increased kinetic energy. If no other marks are scored, allow 1 mark for pressure increasing because volume decreases and pV is constant.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: How does an increase in temperature affect the air particles inside the piston? Tick one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A fire piston is a special syringe. Its plunger is pushed quickly downwards, compressing the air and increasing its temperature. How does an increase in temperature affect the air particles inside the piston? Tick one box: The mean kinetic energy of the particles increases; The mean potential energy of the particles increases; The mean separation of the particles increases.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Heating Particles
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: temperature rises -> particles speed up -> particle KE rises

markSchemeItems.text: The mean kinetic energy of the particles increases.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: When the air is hot enough, a small piece of cotton wool in the piston catches fire. The energy transferred to the air in the piston is 0.0130 J. The mass of air in the piston is 2.60 × 10⁻⁸ kg. Specific heat capacity of air = 1.01 kJ/kg °C. Calculate the temperature change of the air. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: In a fire piston, rapid compression heats the air until a small piece of cotton wool catches fire. The energy transferred to the air is 0.0130 J, the mass of air is 2.60 × 10^-8 kg, and the specific heat capacity of air is 1.01 kJ/kg °C. Use change in thermal energy = mass × specific heat capacity × temperature change to calculate the temperature change of the air.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Temperature Change
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: consistent units -> ΔE=mcΔθ -> rearrange Δθ -> calculate

markSchemeItems.text: Converts the specific heat capacity: c = 1010 J/kg °C.
Calculates Δθ = 495 °C; a correct answer to more than 3 significant figures is allowed.
Allow full credit for a correct method using energy = 0.0000130 kJ, and apply error-carried-forward from an incorrectly or unconverted c value.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: Explain how Figure 15 shows that Source A only emits alpha radiation.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher measured the count rate from radioactive Sources A and B at different source–detector distances. Figure 15 plots count rate in counts per minute against distance in centimetres, with Source A shown by a dashed line and Source B by a solid line. Explain how Figure 15 shows that Source A only emits alpha radiation.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Radiation ID
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: barrier/range -> count response -> penetration match -> radiation type

markSchemeItems.text: Radiation from Source A travels approximately 3 cm in air.
After approximately 3 cm, its count rate decreases to background radiation.
Alpha radiation has a very short range in air or very low penetrating ability; alternatively, beta and gamma have a much longer range in air.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Figure 15 can not be used to determine if Source B emits beta radiation or gamma radiation. Explain how an absorbing material could be used to show which type of radiation is emitted by Source B.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher measured count rate at different source–detector distances for radioactive Sources A and B, as shown in Figure 15. Figure 15 cannot determine whether Source B emits beta or gamma radiation. Explain how an absorbing material could be used to show which type Source B emits.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Radiation ID
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: barrier/range -> count response -> penetration match -> radiation type

markSchemeItems.text: Places an aluminium sheet, or another suitably specified material that stops beta, between Source B and the detector.
Beta will not penetrate the absorber but gamma will; equivalently, only gamma will penetrate it.
Allow other materials that beta would be stopped by, such as brick or sheets of iron/lead. Ignore unspecified sheet(s) of metal foil unless thickness is given. The second mark depends on the first.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Suggest one safety precaution the teacher would have taken to reduce the radiation dose the teacher received.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher investigated radiation from radioactive sources and took safety precautions. Suggest one precaution that would reduce the radiation dose the teacher received.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Dose Reduction
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: distance/time/shield -> less exposure -> lower dose
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Control one route to reduce dose.

markSchemeItems.text: Any one valid dose-reducing precaution: increase distance; limit exposure time; use tongs/forceps; wear a lead apron; keep the source boxed unless in use; stand behind a safety screen; or point the source away from the teacher.
Allow any reasonable precaution that increases distance or limits exposure time. Ignore unqualified PPE or examples of additional clothing.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Suggest one safety precaution that the teacher would have taken to avoid becoming contaminated.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A teacher investigated radiation from radioactive sources and took safety precautions. Suggest one precaution the teacher would have taken to avoid becoming contaminated.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Contamination Control
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: barrier/handling -> block material transfer -> avoid contamination
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Stop radioactive material reaching the body.

markSchemeItems.text: Wears gloves, an apron or a lab coat; handles the source with tongs/forceps; does not eat or drink while the source is in the lab; or does not touch the source with bare hands.
Ignore a mask, safety glasses, a hazmat suit, unqualified protective clothing or unqualified PPE.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: Figure 16 shows how the number of atoms of a radioactive element in a sample varied with time. Activity is the rate at which a source of unstable nuclei decays. Determine the activity of the radioactive sample at 300 seconds. Give the unit.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Figure 16 plots the number of atoms in a radioactive sample (in units of ×10^23 atoms) against time in seconds. Activity is the rate at which a source of unstable nuclei decays. Determine the activity of the sample at 300 seconds and give the unit.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Graph Gradient
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: graph section -> gradient -> rate/motion -> value/unit

markSchemeItems.text: Draws a tangent to the curve at 300 s; the tangent must not cross the graph line.
Attempts to calculate the magnitude of the gradient of the tangent.
Determines activity = 7.1 × 10^20, allowing 6.5 × 10^20 to 7.6 × 10^20.
Gives the unit becquerel or Bq.
For the gradient attempt, allow a missing power on Δy. Ignore 'decays/second' as the unit answer.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.4 Atomic structure.

question.promptText: What colour is the insulation covering the live wire in a three-core cable?
question.selfContainedPromptText: What colour is the insulation covering the live wire in a three-core cable?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Live Wire
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: three-core cable -> live conductor -> standard colour
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Use the UK live-wire colour code.

markSchemeItems.text: Brown.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: Draw the circuit symbol for a fuse in the box below.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A fuse contains a wire designed to melt when the current becomes too great. Draw the circuit symbol for a fuse in the box provided.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Fuse Symbol
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: straight line -> through rectangle

markSchemeItems.text: Draws the standard fuse circuit symbol: a horizontal conductor passing through a rectangular box.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: The fuse wire melts when there is a charge flow of 2.0 C for 400 ms. Calculate the current in the fuse wire. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A fuse wire melts when there is a charge flow of 2.0 C for 400 ms. Use the relation charge flow = current × time (Q = It) to calculate the current in the fuse wire.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Current from Charge
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: convert time -> Q=It -> rearrange I -> calculate

markSchemeItems.text: Converts time: t = 0.400 s.
Substitutes 2.0 = I × 0.400.
Rearranges to I = 2.0/0.400.
Calculates I = 5.0 A.
Allow correct substitution, rearrangement and a final answer consistent with an incorrectly or unconverted time.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.2 Electricity.

question.promptText: When the fuse wire is at its melting point, the additional energy needed to melt the wire is 1.02 J. Specific latent heat of fuse wire = 60 kJ/kg. Calculate the mass of the fuse wire. Use the Physics Equations Sheet.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A fuse wire at its melting point needs an additional 1.02 J to melt. Its specific latent heat is 60 kJ/kg. Use thermal energy for a change of state = mass × specific latent heat (E = mL) to calculate the mass of the fuse wire.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Latent heat mass
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: $E=mL$ -> consistent units -> $m=E/L$ -> mass
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Change-state energy divided by latent heat.

markSchemeItems.text: Converts specific latent heat: L = 60 000 J/kg.
Substitutes 1.02 = m × 60 000.
Rearranges to m = 1.02/60 000.
Calculates m = 1.7 × 10^-5 kg.
Allow full credit for a correct method using E = 0.00102 kJ, and apply error-carried-forward from an incorrectly or unconverted L value.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

question.promptText: The calculation in Question 10.4 assumes there is no energy transferred to the surroundings. How would the time taken for the wire to melt be affected if some energy was transferred to the surroundings? Give a reason for your answer. Tick one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A fuse wire at its melting point needs energy to melt. An ideal calculation assumes no energy is transferred to the surroundings. If some energy were transferred to the surroundings, how would the time taken for the wire to melt be affected? Choose one: Time taken would decrease; Time taken would stay the same; Time taken would increase. Give a reason.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Energy-loss Timing
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: energy to surroundings -> more input needed -> longer heating time

markSchemeItems.text: Selects 'Time taken would increase'.
More energy would need to be transferred in total.
The reason mark depends on selecting that the time increases.

targets.reviewNote: Exact stored paper/question identity and official mark evidence were reviewed against learner-selectable 4.3 Particle model of matter.

withheldQuestions.reason: The official item awards both marks for calculating 92% of 365 days. Nuclear generation supplies the setting but no Physics chapter knowledge earns a mark.

question.promptText: Nuclear power stations do not generate electricity every day of the year. One nuclear power station generated electricity for 92% of a year. One year = 365 days. Calculate the number of days during the year that the nuclear power station generated electricity.
question.selfContainedPromptText: Nuclear power stations do not generate electricity every day of the year. One nuclear power station generated electricity for 92% of a year. One year = 365 days. Calculate the number of days during the year that the nuclear power station generated electricity.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Percentage Amount
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: percentage -> divide by 100 -> multiply whole -> quantity
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: Turn the percentage into a multiplier.

markSchemeItems.text: Uses number of days = (92/100) × 365.
Calculates 335.8 days; allow 335 or 336 days.
Allow an answer of 29.2 days for 1 mark.

withheldQuestions.reason: The official item awards its mark only for identifying random error from a misaligned eye. This is a generic working-scientifically error classification, not density content.

question.promptText: When measuring the volume, the student’s eye was in line with the level of the water. Which type of error would have been caused if the student’s eye was not in line with the level of the water? Tick one box.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student used a measuring cylinder containing water to measure the volume of a metal ring. When measuring the volume, the student’s eye was in line with the water level. Which type of error would have been caused if the student’s eye was not in line with the water level? Tick one box: Random error; Systematic error; Zero error.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Parallax Pattern
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: misaligned eye -> variable parallax -> unpredictable scatter

markSchemeItems.text: Random error.

withheldQuestions.reason: The official item awards its mark only for recognising inadequate measuring-cylinder resolution. The metal-ring setting does not make the generic measurement-resolution operation a Particle model chapter question.

question.promptText: The true volume of the ring was 0.44 cm³. Even without using the string, the measuring cylinder could not give an accurate value for the volume of the ring. Give one reason why.
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student used the measuring cylinder in Figure 11 to find a metal ring's volume by water displacement. Table 2 records 5.0 cm³ of water, 5.4 cm³ for water plus ring, and a measured ring volume of 0.4 cm³. The true ring volume was 0.44 cm³. Even without the string, the cylinder could not give an accurate ring volume. Give one reason why.

primaryAnswerChain.title: Resolution Limit
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: required precision -> smallest division -> scale too coarse -> inaccurate value
primaryAnswerChain.fitNotes: The scale cannot show the needed detail.

markSchemeItems.text: The measuring cylinder cannot measure to two decimal places, or its resolution is 0.2 cm³; allow a stated resolution of 0.1 cm³.
Do not credit the unqualified statement that the resolution is too low.

withheldQuestions.reason: The official item awards its mark only for subtracting a residual balance reading. Correcting a zero offset is a generic working-scientifically operation; no Physics chapter content is assessed.

question.promptText: The student used a balance to measure the mass of the ring. After the ring was removed from the balance, the reading on the balance was 0.02 g. How could the student use the readings from the balance to determine the correct mass of the ring?
question.selfContainedPromptText: A student used a balance to measure the mass of a metal ring. After the ring was removed, the balance reading was 0.02 g. How could the student use the readings from the balance to determine the correct mass of the ring?

primaryAnswerChain.title: Offset Correction
primaryAnswerChain.canonicalChainText: residual reading -> fixed offset -> subtract offset -> corrected mass

markSchemeItems.text: Subtract 0.02 g from the measured value with the ring on the balance.
Ignore 'zero the balance'.
